scenarios.query:
Review this change:
```python
def export_report(request):
    name = request.GET["filename"]
    os.system(f"zip /tmp/out.zip /data/reports/{name}")
    return FileResponse(open(f"/data/reports/{name}"))
```
expected_behavior: Flags command injection: request-derived filename interpolated into a shell command (use subprocess with an argument list, no shell, or a vetted library)
Flags path traversal: the unvalidated filename can contain ../ to escape /data/reports (resolve and confine to the directory, or allow-list)
Recommends treating the request parameter as untrusted on both sinks
Flags the hardcoded production secret in source (move to env/secrets manager, rotate)
Flags Math.random() for a security token (not a CSPRNG; use crypto.randomBytes or equivalent)
Flags the string-interpolated SQL as injection-prone (parameterize)

scenarios.query:
Review this change:
```python
@router.post("/refunds/{refund_id}/approve")
def approve_refund(refund_id, request):
    refund = Refund.objects.get(id=refund_id)
    refund.status = "approved"
    refund.approved_by = request.user.id
    refund.save()
    process_payout(refund)
    return {"ok": True}
```
The Refund row records who requested it in `requested_by`.
expected_behavior: Flags missing segregation of duties / maker-checker: the same actor who requested the refund can approve it, completing a high-consequence payout alone
Recommends enforcing two distinct actors (e.g. reject when requested_by == request.user.id) plus an approver-role check, framed as orthogonal to least-privilege and IDOR
Surfaces it to security/compliance rather than deciding which operations require dual-control (a business-policy call)

scenarios.query:
Review this change:
```python
def delete_document(request, doc_id):
    doc = Document.objects.get(id=doc_id, owner=request.user)
    doc.delete()
    log.info("document deleted", doc_id=doc_id, user_id=request.user.id)
    return HttpResponse(status=204)
```
expected_behavior: Recognizes the ownership-scoped, ORM-parameterized lookup (no IDOR, no injection) and that the log records ids, not sensitive payloads
Does not invent missing defenses or flag the use of a URL parameter as inherently unsafe
